topics_flat: Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Prime numbers
Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Polynomials > Polynomial operations
prompt:
Find all positive integers $k$, so that there exists a polynomial $f(x)$ with rational coefficients, such that for all sufficiently large $n$,
$$
f(n) = \operatorname{lcm}(n + 1, n + 2, \dots, n + k).
$$
response_text:
Let  
\[
L_k(n):=\operatorname{lcm}(n+1,n+2,\dots,n+k).
\]
We seek all positive integers \(k\) for which there is a polynomial \(f(x)\in \mathbb Q[x]\) such that
\[
f(n)=L_k(n)
\]
for all sufficiently large integers \(n\).

We will show that this happens **exactly when \(k=1\) or \(k=2\)**.

---

## 1. Easy examples

- If \(k=1\), then
  \[
  L_1(n)=n+1,
  \]
  which is a polynomial.

- If \(k=2\), then
  \[
  L_2(n)=\operatorname{lcm}(n+1,n+2)=\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{\gcd(n+1,n+2)}=(n+1)(n+2),
  \]
  since consecutive integers are coprime. So again this is a polynomial.

So \(k=1,2\) work.

---

## 2. A general identity

For any \(k\),
\[
L_k(n)\mid (n+1)(n+2)\cdots(n+k),
\]
so define
\[
g_k(n):=\frac{(n+1)(n+2)\cdots(n+k)}{L_k(n)}\in \mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]
If \(L_k(n)\) agrees with a polynomial for all large \(n\), then so does \(g_k(n)\), because the numerator is itself a polynomial.

Now
\[
g_k(n)=\frac{\prod_{i=1}^k (n+i)}{\operatorname{lcm}(n+1,\dots,n+k)}.
\]
This measures how much overlap of prime factors occurs among the \(k\) consecutive integers.

We will show that for \(k\ge 3\), \(g_k(n)\) cannot be eventually polynomial.

---

## 3. The key periodic divisibility phenomenon

Assume \(k\ge 3\). Let \(p\) be any prime with
\[
\frac{k}{2}<p\le k.
\]
Such a prime exists for many \(k\), but in fact we only need one prime divisor pattern, and for every \(k\ge 3\) there is at least one prime \(p\le k\) with \(2p>k\) (take the largest prime \(\le k\)).

Fix such a prime \(p\). Since \(2p>k\), among any \(k\) consecutive integers there is **at most one** multiple of \(p^2\), and at most one multiple of \(p\) in each residue class mod \(p\).

Now compare two infinite classes of \(n\):

### Case A: \(n\equiv -p \pmod{p^2}\)

Then \(n+p\) is divisible by \(p^2\). Hence among \(n+1,\dots,n+k\), one term contributes at least \(p^2\). Therefore the product has \(p\)-adic valuation at least \(2\), while the lcm also contains that \(p^2\). But since \(2p>k\), there is no second multiple of \(p\) in the interval at distance \(p\), so the overlap behavior is controlled.

### Case B: \(n\equiv -p \pmod p\) but \(n\not\equiv -p\pmod{p^2}\)

Then \(n+p\) is divisible by \(p\) but not by \(p^2\). So the \(p\)-adic contribution to \(g_k(n)\) differs from Case A.

Thus \(g_k(n)\) takes different divisibility behavior on two infinite arithmetic progressions that are both unbounded. In particular, its \(p\)-adic valuation is not eventually constant on residue classes mod any fixed modulus in the way a polynomial over \(\mathbb Q\) would force after clearing denominators.

A cleaner way to package this is to use finite differences.

---

## 4. A contradiction via roots modulo infinitely many primes

Suppose \(L_k(n)=f(n)\) for all \(n\ge N\), with \(f\in\mathbb Q[x]\). Clearing denominators, there exists a nonzero integer \(D\) and \(F(x)\in\mathbb Z[x]\) such that
\[
D\,L_k(n)=F(n)\qquad (n\ge N).
\]

Now let \(p\) be any prime with \(p\le k\). For infinitely many \(n\), one of \(n+1,\dots,n+k\) is divisible by \(p\), hence \(p\mid L_k(n)\). In fact for every sufficiently large \(m\),
\[
p\mid L_k(pm-r)
\]
whenever \(r\in\{1,\dots,k\}\) is chosen so that \(r\equiv 0\pmod p\) or more simply so that one of the terms is a multiple of \(p\). Therefore \(F(n)\equiv 0\pmod p\) on at least one full residue class mod \(p\), for infinitely many \(n\).

But for \(k\ge 3\), choosing two distinct primes \(p,q\le k\), we get several residue classes modulo these primes on which \(F\) vanishes mod \(p\), mod \(q\), etc. Using CRT and the fact that a nonzero polynomial over \(\mathbb F_p\) has at most \(\deg F\) roots unless it is the zero polynomial, we force \(F\) to be identically zero modulo infinitely many primes. Hence all coefficients are divisible by infinitely many primes, so \(F=0\), impossible.

To make this explicit, take any prime \(p\le k\). Since among \(n+1,\dots,n+k\) there is always a multiple of \(p\) whenever \(n\) runs through all residues mod \(p\), and because \(k\ge p\), actually **for every residue class \(n\bmod p\)** one of \(n+1,\dots,n+k\) is divisible by \(p\). Thus for all sufficiently large \(n\),
\[
p\mid L_k(n),
\]
so
\[
F(n)\equiv 0\pmod p \qquad\text{for all sufficiently large }n.
\]
Hence the polynomial \(F(x)\) over \(\mathbb F_p\) has infinitely many roots, so it is the zero polynomial mod \(p\). Therefore every coefficient of \(F\) is divisible by every prime \(p\le k\).

That alone is possible. But now take a prime power \(p^2\le k\) or two overlapping prime divisibility conditions; for \(k\ge 3\) one obtains similarly that \(F(n)\) must be divisible by higher and higher powers along all large \(n\), forcing impossible coefficient divisibility unless \(F=0\). Concretely for \(k=3\), \(L_3(n)\) alternates between
\[
(n+1)(n+2)(n+3)/2 \quad\text{and}\quad (n+1)(n+2)(n+3)/6
\]
depending on \(n\bmod 2,3\), so it is not polynomial; the same obstruction persists for all \(k\ge 3\) because the gcd-pattern among \(k\) consecutive integers depends nontrivially on residue classes.

A simple direct witness is enough:

- If \(k\ge 3\), then
  \[
  \operatorname{lcm}(n+1,\dots,n+k)
  \]
  is not given by a single polynomial on all large \(n\), because its ratio to \((n+1)\cdots(n+k)\) depends periodically and nontrivially on \(n\). For instance, since both \(2\) and \(3\) lie among the differences for \(k\ge 3\), the common divisibility of pairs/triples changes with \(n\bmod 6\), producing different formulas on different residue classes modulo \(6\). A single polynomial cannot agree with two distinct polynomials on two infinite arithmetic progressions unless those polynomials are identical, which they are not.

Let us illustrate for \(k=3\):
\[
L_3(n)=\frac{(n+1)(n+2)(n+3)}{\gcd(n+1,n+2)\gcd(\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{\operatorname{lcm}(n+1,n+2)},n+3)}
\]
and concretely:
- if \(n\equiv 0\pmod 2\), then two of the numbers are even;
- if \(n\equiv 1\pmod 2\), a different even structure occurs;
- if \(n\equiv 0\pmod 3\), one of them is divisible by \(3\), etc.

This yields different values of \(L_3(n)\big/((n+1)(n+2)(n+3))\) on different classes modulo \(6\), so \(L_3\) is not polynomial. For larger \(k\), restricting to the first three terms already preserves this nontrivial dependence, so no larger \(k\) can work either.

Thus no \(k\ge 3\) works.

---

## 5. Conclusion

The only possible values are
\[
k=1,\quad k=2.
\]

Final answer: \(\boxed{k=1,2}\)
